summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – ADOPTION – Whether leave to commence adoption proceedings is in the best interests of the child– “prescribed adopting parent” – Leave granted under s 60G of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) to commence proceedings. PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Where a registrar ordered joinder and service upon deceased father as a respondent – where the application is irregularly constituted – where further orders for service did not stipulate who the applicants were to serve – where the order designating the deceased as a respondent was wrong – order set aside.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

This is an application pursuant to s 60G(1) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) for leave to adopt a child, X, born in 2010. The applicants are his mother, Ms Domna and her husband, Mr Domna. X's father, Mr B, died in 2019. I am satisfied by the evidence of his death. In those circumstances, as the applicants explain in their material, there is no respondent to these proceedings.
X presently lives with the applicants. As explained in any number of previous decisions of this court, although the process of adoption occurs as an application of Queensland law by the relevant state authorities, in the case of adoption by a step-parent, a necessary first step is to seek leave to adopt from this court. A step-parent may only apply for adoption of a child if they have first been granted leave to adopt the child pursuant to s 60G(1) of the Act: s 92(1)(d) of the Adoption Act 2009 (Qld).
As Carew J recently observed in Denforth & Sable [2023] FedCFamC1F 903 at paragraph 3:
Since 1 September 2021, the jurisdiction of this Court to determine an application for leave to commence adoption proceedings is dependent upon the proceedings being transferred from an inferior Court - namely, the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) (formerly known as the Federal Circuit Court of Australia), a court of record - to this Court (formerly known as the Family Court of Australia), a superior court of record (see section 9(1)(a) and section 10(1)(a) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021). Since 1 September 2021, the proceedings can no longer be instituted in this Court - but, because of a protocol between the Courts, such applications are generally transferred, such is the importance given to the jurisdiction relating to adoption.
Her Honour went on to set out the less-than-straightforward statutory arrangements by which this Court comes to have jurisdiction in applications such as the present. Those remarks by her Honour are to be found in paragraphs 4, 5, 6 and 7 of her judgment.
The present application was commenced in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) on 12 September 2025 and is presently continued by the amended application for final orders filed on 7 November 2025. The application was transferred to this Court by an order of a deputy registrar made on 19 November 2025.
As a Court of limited jurisdiction, I am obliged to ensure that I have jurisdiction to deal with the application before me. Having regard to the facts that I have just set out, I am satisfied that I am seized of jurisdiction in the application.
The discretion reposed in the Court by s 60G(1) of the Act is enlivened when the application is made by a prescribed adopting parent. Mr Domna is a prescribed adopting parent. So, too, is the second applicant, Ms Domna.
By s 60G(2) of the Act, the discretion to grant leave is informed by - amongst other things ‑ whether granting leave would be in the child's best interests, having regard to the effect of section 60F(4)(a) or section 60HA(3)(a) and of section 61E and section 65J of the Act.
Section 60F(4)(a) provides that a child who would otherwise be considered a child of a marriage as prescribed by s 60F(1)(a) of the Act, is no longer a child of that marriage where the child is adopted and leave under s 60G(1) of Act was granted in respect of that child. As the second applicant and the purported respondent were married at the time of X’s birth, he is a child of their marriage. If I grant leave to adopt and she is adopted by the first applicant, he will cease to be a child of the marriage between the second applicant and the purported respondent. I take that into account.
Section 60H(3)(a) has no application here because it applies where the parents of the child sought to be adopted were not married but in a de facto relationship (see s 60HA(1) of the Act).
Section 61E deals with the effect of adoption upon a person’s parental responsibility for the child adopted. If there has been a grant of leave under s 60G(2) of the Act then the effect of an adoption will be to remove from a person who had parental responsibility for the child adopted, that parental responsibility. A literal reading of s 61E(2) of the Act would mean that in this case, the second applicant’s and the respondent’s parental responsibility for X will end upon his adoption by the first applicant (see Cahill & Fryar [2008] FamCA 1245 at [13], Brock & Brock [2007] FamCA 1594 at [12], Tanner & Cape [2011] FamCA 665 at [15] and the other cases referred to therein). Despite the text of s 61E(2), in Hanlon & Hanlon [2022] FedCFamC1F 435, Gill J concluded that on its proper construction, s 61E of the Act did not operate to strip parental responsibility from the parent who was partnered with the adopting step-parent (see also Haskova & Viraj [2025] FedCFamC1F 103, at [19]).
I accept that the views expressed in Hanlon and Haskova are the preferable views of the work done by s 61E(2) of the Act and in the event that X is adopted by the first applicant, only the purported respondent’s parental responsibility for him would end if he was alive, not that of the second applicant. I take that into account.
The evidence is that the applicants married in 2021. They have another child together, C, who was born in 2020 and so X has a half-sibling.
There is a parenting order in place in respect of X. It provides the second applicant, Ms Domna, with sole parental responsibility for X. I take that into account although in circumstances where the purported respondent to that order, Mr B has died, the order is now of little consequence.
Notwithstanding the breakdown in the relationship between Mr B's father and the second applicant here and his subsequent death, X retains a relationship with his paternal grandfather, Mr D and paternal great-grandmother, Mrs E. I have evidence from them and they support these proceedings and the orders sought by the applicants in them. The evidence satisfies me that X has, and continues to maintain, a close relationship with those people.
The evidence demonstrates a close family connection between the applicants, X and a wider cohort of family. The second applicant's affidavit, in particular, sets out those close relationships.
Having regard to the evidence of Mr Domna set out in his affidavit and the evidence of Ms Domna set out in her two affidavits, I am satisfied, pursuant to s 60CC of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), that it is in the best interests of X for there to be an order under s 60G(1) of the Family Law Act.

POST SCRIPT

Since delivering reasons in open court when I heard this application, the constitution of the proceedings was brought to my attention. The application is irregularly constituted and requires regularisation. The difficulty arises in the following way.
After the commencement of these proceedings, they were listed before a deputy registrar, in chambers, on 29 October 2025. The deputy registrar made these orders:
By no later than 4.00pm AEST on 12 November 2025, the First Applicant must file and serve an Amended Initiating Application (Family Law) including the biological Father (deceased) as the Respondent setting out with precision the Final Orders sought..
By no later than 4.00pm AEST on 12 November 2025, the Second Applicant must file and serve a further Affidavit setting out the particulars of her relationship with the biological Father, her relationship with the First Applicant and the Child’s relationship with the First Respondent.
By no later than 4.00pm AEST on 12 November 2025, the First Applicant must file and serve a further Affidavit setting out the particulars of his knowledge of the relationship between the biological Father and the Child and his own relationship with the Child.
(headings and remaining orders omitted)
The purported respondent, the biological father of the child, Mr B died in 2019 well before the proceedings commenced. That fact was clearly stated in the affidavit of the second applicant filed with the Initiating Application on 12 September, 2025.
Orders 1, 2 and 3 set out above require service of the amended Initiating Application and the affidavits required by orders 2 and 3 to be served. But the order is silent as to who is to be served with those documents. It would be pointless to have the applicants serve the documents upon themselves. The purported respondent could not be served as he had ceased to be.
This court derives its jurisdiction and power from statute. As held by the Full Court In the Marriage of Sims [1981] FamCA 102; (1981) 7 Fam LR 667 (at 674):
Save as expressly provided this statute does not confer any general power on this court to entertain proceedings against or by the legal personal representative of a deceased party nor is there power to continue proceedings after the death of a party by substituting that party's personal representative as a party in lieu of the deceased party.
The order to join X’s father as a respondent to the proceedings was wrong. A fortiori the order to serve him with documents. The joinder to parenting proceedings of a deceased person, either in the way it was purported to be done here, or by a legal personal representative, is not authorised by the Act or the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth). Rule 3.02 provides no authority for the joinder of a deceased person to proceedings. For an example of a provision authorising the continuation of proceedings against the legal personal representative of a party who dies in the course of proceedings, see s 79AA(8) of the Act.
The orders made on 29 October 2025 were made by a Judicial Registrar of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) exercising delegated power pursuant to s 254(2) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) and Schedule 4 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) as modified by the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth). Whilst s 256(2) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) provides a power to review the exercise of a delegated power, the relevant review power can only be exercised by a judge of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2). A similar power of review reposes in judges of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1), but only in respect of the exercise of a powers delegated by judges of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1). I cannot exercise, on my own motion, a power of review in respect of the erroneous orders.
However, r 10.13(1) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) supplies the necessary power to set aside the orders made on 29 October 2025. Pursuant to that rule, the court may at any time set aside an order, if it was made in the absence of a party (r 10.13(1)(a)) or it is interlocutory (r 10.13(1)(c)). The order here was made in the absence of the applicants and it was interlocutory. I set it aside because to make it was an error of law.
Some consequential orders are necessary to regularise the court record. The proceedings should be reconstituted by the omission of the purported respondent. It is unnecessary to make an order removing the respondent from the proceedings, he having ceased to be. I have set the necessary orders out at the commencement of these reasons. Otherwise, all necessary administrative modifications should be made by the Registrar of the Court to ensure that the court record reflects these reasons.
